metadata.seed: Um, I don't know. Maybe I am. Maybe I don't deserve to be there.

patient.query: Um, I don't know. Maybe I am. Maybe I don't deserve to be there.

automatic_thoughts: I don't deserve to be there

neutral: Fear of being in a small group (finding)

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re feeling unsure about whether you belong here, and that uncertainty can be really tough. What thoughts or experiences are making you feel that you might not deserve to be here?

retrieved_concepts_used.concept: I don't deserve to be there
retrieved_concepts_used.label: automatic_thoughts

patient.query: Yeah, I guess that's true. I often feel like I'm just... not enough, you know? Like everyone else has their lives together, and I’m just pretending to be okay. It makes me second-guess why I'm even trying to work on myself.

automatic_thoughts: I'm not enough
Everyone else has their lives together
I'm just pretending to be okay
Why am I even trying to work on myself?

entailment: Fear of being a burden to others (finding)

llm_response.response: I hear you feeling like you’re not enough and that everyone else seems to have it all figured out. What evidence do you have that supports that view? Are there moments when you’ve felt competent or genuine?

patient.query: It’s just... I see people around me succeeding, and it feels like I'm always falling short in comparison. Sometimes, I manage to do something well, maybe at work or with friends, but it never seems to last. I end up feeling like those moments were just lucky breaks, not something I earned.

automatic_thoughts: I'm always falling short
my successes are just lucky breaks
I don't deserve my achievements

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re feeling that your achievements are fleeting and not truly yours. What evidence do you have that these moments were just luck rather than the result of your effort? How might you view those successes if you considered them as part of your growing skill set?

patient.query: I guess I just don’t see the skills in myself that other people do. When I think about those successful moments, it feels like I was just in the right place at the right time. I really want to feel proud of myself, but instead, I end up pushing those thoughts away, like they don’t count somehow. It’s frustrating.

automatic_thoughts: I don’t see the skills in myself that other people do
I was just in the right place at the right time
Those successful moments don’t count

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re feeling frustrated because you think your successes are just luck and that you don’t see your own skills. What moments come to mind where you felt you actually contributed to those outcomes? How might you view those moments if you considered them as evidence of your abilities rather than just being in the right place at the right time?

patient.query: Yeah, those moments do pop up sometimes. Like that time I led a project at work and it went well, or when a friend said I really helped them through a tough time. But it’s hard to hold onto that feeling. I just struggle to believe it was me who made it happen, you know? I kind of brush it off and focus on all the things I think I did wrong instead.

automatic_thoughts: I struggle to believe it was me
I focus on all the things I did wrong

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re having a hard time accepting the positive feedback you’ve received. What specific thoughts or memories make you doubt that your actions were the cause of those successes? Are there any details that might suggest you did play a role in making it happen?

patient.query: Yeah, I guess I just keep thinking back to times when I failed or messed things up. It makes it hard to see those good moments as really being my doing. I remember the project went well, but I feel like anyone could have stepped in and done the same job. I don't know… I keep thinking I just got lucky or that people were just being nice when they praised me. It's like my brain doesn’t want to let me believe I actually contributed something meaningful.

automatic_thoughts: I failed or messed things up in the past
I didn't really do anything special
I just got lucky
People were just being nice when they praised me

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re wrestling with doubts about your own contributions. What specific moments or feedback do you recall that might suggest you had a meaningful role? If you were talking to a supportive friend, how might they describe your impact on the project?

patient.query: I remember my coworkers saying I organized everything really well and that it helped the team stay on track. I suppose if a friend were describing it, they might say I brought some good ideas to the table and kept everyone motivated. But even hearing that, I still can’t shake the feeling that it was just a group effort where I happened to be there. It’s like I’m not allowing myself to own it.

automatic_thoughts: it was just a group effort
I happened to be there
I'm not allowing myself to own it

neutral: Character trait finding of personal organization (finding)
Fear of being in a small group (finding)
Character trait finding of effort at work (finding)

llm_response.response: I hear you feeling unsure about taking credit for the project. What specific moments or actions do you think show that you contributed to the team's success? How might you view your role if you considered both the teamwork and your own input?

patient.query: I don’t know... I guess I did come up with some plans and kept the communication going. Maybe because I was the one who suggested a few key ideas, but it still feels weird to say that. I just feel like I was part of a team doing its job, and it almost feels selfish to think I played a big role. It’s like I can’t let myself feel good about it without worrying that I’m exaggerating my own importance. It’s confusing.

automatic_thoughts: I can't let myself feel good about it
I'm exaggerating my own importance

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re feeling a mix of pride and discomfort about your contributions, and that’s a common reaction when you’re part of a team effort. Balancing the desire to feel good about what you’ve done with the worry that you might be overestimating your role can feel confusing. You’re not alone in experiencing that tension.

patient.query: Yeah, that does seem pretty accurate. I guess part of me wants to celebrate those moments but then another part feels like I shouldn’t. It's like I’m scared of being seen as someone who thinks too highly of themselves. I want to feel confident but then I just end up doubting everything, including my worth. It’s like there's this constant battle in my head.

automatic_thoughts: I shouldn’t celebrate my moments
I’m scared of being seen as arrogant
I doubt my worth

llm_response.response: It sounds like there’s a tension between wanting to recognize your successes and fearing how others might see that. What evidence do you have that celebrating your moments would make you appear arrogant? Conversely, what evidence might suggest that sharing your achievements could be seen as confidence rather than pride?

patient.query: I guess I don’t really have solid proof that celebrating would look bad... it’s more this feeling I have. I've seen people talk about others being too self-promoting or full of themselves, so I worry that if I share my success, I could come off like that. But on the flip side, I see people celebrating and they seem to be inspiring others, not pushing them away. It just feels risky to put myself out there like that.

automatic_thoughts: celebrating would look bad
I could come off as self-promoting
putting myself out there feels risky

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re balancing the fear of being seen as self‑promoting with the possibility of inspiring others. What evidence do you have that sharing your success would be perceived negatively? And what evidence supports the idea that it could be viewed positively?
